expectations.text: The 'description:' field in the SKILL.md frontmatter contains an explicit exclusion clause using the literal phrase 'NOT for' (capitalized exactly that way) followed by at least one excluded use case — not just a description of what the skill does.
expectations.evidence: The description field reads: '...Do NOT use for: generating schemas from data, documenting APIs, or converting between data formats.' The phrase present is 'NOT use for' (with 'use' between NOT and for), not the literal consecutive phrase 'NOT for'. The assertion requires 'NOT for' capitalized exactly that way as a consecutive phrase.

expectations.text: The SKILL.md body contains at least one explicit anti-pattern block with three labeled subheadings drawn from {Symptom, Problem, Solution} OR {What it looks like, Why it's wrong, What to do instead} — each labeled and on its own line/bold heading, naming a specific JSON-validation pitfall (e.g., schema drift, silent parse errors, validating untrusted input, missing required fields).
expectations.evidence: The SKILL.md body contains sections: 'When to Use', 'When NOT to Use', 'Implementation' (8 steps with code), 'Error Handling' (a markdown table with columns Error | Cause | Fix), and 'Example Invocations'. The Error Handling table lists scenarios like 'SyntaxError: Unexpected token', 'schema $schema unknown', etc., but these are table rows, not labeled subheadings on their own bold lines. No anti-pattern block using the required {Symptom/Problem/Solution} or {What it looks like/Why it's wrong/What to do instead} format exists anywhere in the body.

expectations.text: The SKILL.md contains BOTH a positive use list AND a separate negative list (e.g., a 'When to Use' section with '✅ Use for:' and '❌ NOT for:' bullets, OR distinct 'When to Use' and 'When NOT to Use' sections) — not merely a single 'Use this for X' paragraph.
expectations.evidence: The body contains a distinct '## When to Use' section with four bullet points (validating config files, API payloads, data exports, CI gate) and a separate '## When NOT to Use' section with three bullet points (generating schemas from data, writing API reference docs, converting JSON to another format). These are clearly distinct sections, not a single paragraph.

expectations.text: audit.md explicitly flags the description as missing an exclusion / NOT-for clause AND as missing specific trigger keywords (using language like 'NOT for', 'no exclusion', 'no boundaries', 'lacks specific keywords', or 'too vague to trigger reliably').
expectations.evidence: Issue 6 in output.txt: 'Missing exclusion clause — No "when NOT to use" section; dispatcher will over-trigger on docs/config/formatting changes.' covers the exclusion/NOT-for clause. Issue 3: 'Circular description — "Use this skill to review code" restates the description. Rewrite to give the dispatcher a trigger signal.' covers the missing-trigger-keywords dimension (implies description is too vague to trigger reliably).

expectations.text: audit.md identifies the broken reference to `references/full-guide.md` by filename or path AND labels it with one of: 'phantom', 'orphaned', 'broken', 'non-existent', 'dangling', 'doesn't exist', or 'reference illusion'.
expectations.evidence: Issue 1 in output.txt: 'Dead reference — `references/full-guide.md` doesn't exist; step 4 is a dead end.' Names the path explicitly and uses 'doesn't exist', which is on the required-label list.

expectations.text: audit.md recommends adding at least one of: an explicit anti-pattern section with Symptom/Problem/Solution (or What/Why/Instead) structure, a Philosophy section that comes before the procedural steps, OR shibboleths / expert-vs-novice framing — named explicitly (the words 'anti-pattern', 'philosophy', or 'shibboleth' must appear).
expectations.evidence: The six issues in output.txt address dead references, blocked tools, circular description, non-actionable steps, generic tips, and missing exclusion clause. None use the words 'anti-pattern', 'philosophy', or 'shibboleth'. The closest is Issue 5 ('Generic tips — "Be thorough / Consider edge cases / Check tests" apply to everything. Replace with code-review-specific heuristics.') but it does not recommend the specifically named structural improvements required.

expectations.text: fix.md contains a rewritten description that includes at least TWO specific trigger keywords from the user's stated scope (any two of: 'OpenAPI', 'function signatures', 'parameter tables', 'type signatures', 'API reference') AND an explicit 'NOT for' clause naming at least TWO excluded categories from the user's list (any two of: blog posts, marketing copy, README, README files, general writing).
expectations.evidence: fix.md YAML description includes 'OpenAPI/Swagger spec files', 'function and method signature blocks', 'parameter tables', 'type signatures', and 'SDK reference pages' (all from the trigger keyword list). The explicit exclusion clause reads: 'Does NOT handle blog posts, README prose, marketing copy, tutorials, changelogs, or general writing tasks.' — naming 'blog posts', 'README prose', 'marketing copy', and 'general writing tasks' from the excluded categories list.

expectations.text: fix.md recommends a concrete activation-testing approach, satisfied by EITHER (a) naming a skill-foundry script by filename — one of `test_activation.py`, `quick_validate.py`, `validate_skill.py`, `analyze_skill.py` — OR (b) listing at least 3 positive trigger prompts AND at least 2 negative trigger prompts to test against.
expectations.evidence: fix.md includes a 'Test battery' section with 6 true-positive prompts (TP-1 through TP-6) and 8 false-positive guard prompts (FP-1 through FP-8), satisfying option (b). Example TPs: 'Generate an OpenAPI 3.1 spec for our /users/{id} endpoint', 'Write function signatures with JSDoc parameter tables for our auth module'. Example FPs: 'Write a blog post about our new authentication system', 'Create a README for this repository'.

expectations.text: fix.md uses at least one skill-foundry concept by name — one of: 'progressive disclosure', 'shibboleth', 'shibboleths', 'philosophy-first', 'description engineering', or 'Description Field Engineering'.
expectations.evidence: The full fix.md content was examined. None of the required skill-foundry concept names appear: 'progressive disclosure', 'shibboleth', 'shibboleths', 'philosophy-first', 'description engineering', or 'Description Field Engineering'. The document uses its own vocabulary ('domain vocabulary', 'concrete artifact names', 'explicit exclusions', 'action verbs tied to the domain') but does not reference any named skill-foundry concepts.

expectations.text: The 'description:' field in the SKILL.md frontmatter contains an explicit exclusion clause using the literal phrase 'NOT for' (capitalized exactly that way) followed by at least one excluded use case — not just a description of what the skill does.
expectations.evidence: The description field reads: 'Do NOT use for generating schemas, creating API documentation, or general JSON formatting tasks.' It uses 'Do NOT use for' rather than the literal phrase 'NOT for'. The phrase 'NOT for' (NOT directly followed by 'for') does not appear in the description field.

expectations.text: The SKILL.md body contains at least one explicit anti-pattern block with three labeled subheadings drawn from {Symptom, Problem, Solution} OR {What it looks like, Why it's wrong, What to do instead} — each labeled and on its own line/bold heading, naming a specific JSON-validation pitfall (e.g., schema drift, silent parse errors, validating untrusted input, missing required fields).
expectations.evidence: The SKILL.md body contains: 'When to use', 'When NOT to use', 'Steps', 'Node.js / Bun example', 'Python example', 'Batch validation', 'Error handling' (a table with 5 rows), and 'Output format'. There is no anti-pattern block with the required three-subheading structure ({Symptom/Problem/Solution} or {What it looks like/Why it's wrong/What to do instead}).

expectations.text: The SKILL.md contains BOTH a positive use list AND a separate negative list (e.g., a 'When to Use' section with '✅ Use for:' and '❌ NOT for:' bullets, OR distinct 'When to Use' and 'When NOT to Use' sections) — not merely a single 'Use this for X' paragraph.
expectations.evidence: The SKILL.md has a '## When to use' section with 4 bullet points (e.g., 'User provides a JSON file and a schema file and asks to validate') and a separate '## When NOT to use' section with 3 bullet points (e.g., 'Generating a JSON Schema from sample data → use a schema-inference tool instead'). These are distinct sections satisfying the requirement.

expectations.text: audit.md explicitly flags the description as missing an exclusion / NOT-for clause AND as missing specific trigger keywords (using language like 'NOT for', 'no exclusion', 'no boundaries', 'lacks specific keywords', or 'too vague to trigger reliably').
expectations.evidence: Issue 6 in output.txt says '**Missing exclusion clause** — no guidance on when to skip the skill. Fix: add a "When NOT to use" section' — this covers the exclusion/NOT-for clause part. However, the audit does NOT explicitly flag the description as missing specific trigger keywords. Issue 3 says '**Circular description** — both the frontmatter and body just restate the skill name. Fix: describe the six review dimensions and the trigger condition.' While it mentions 'trigger condition' in the fix, it does not use any of the required language ('lacks specific keywords', 'too vague to trigger reliably', 'no boundaries', etc.) to call out missing trigger keywords as a distinct problem. The AND condition fails.

expectations.text: audit.md identifies the broken reference to `references/full-guide.md` by filename or path AND labels it with one of: 'phantom', 'orphaned', 'broken', 'non-existent', 'dangling', 'doesn't exist', or 'reference illusion'.
expectations.evidence: Issue 1 in output.txt: '**Dead reference** — `references/full-guide.md` doesn't exist; step 4 is a dead end.' The path `references/full-guide.md` is cited explicitly, and the phrase "doesn't exist" is one of the accepted labels.

expectations.text: audit.md recommends adding at least one of: an explicit anti-pattern section with Symptom/Problem/Solution (or What/Why/Instead) structure, a Philosophy section that comes before the procedural steps, OR shibboleths / expert-vs-novice framing — named explicitly (the words 'anti-pattern', 'philosophy', or 'shibboleth' must appear).
expectations.evidence: Grep for 'anti-pattern', 'philosophy', and 'shibboleth' in the transcript returned no matches. The output.txt lists six issues and none of them mention anti-patterns, a philosophy section, or shibboleths. The recommendations focus on dead references, blocked tools, circular description, non-actionable steps, generic tips, and a missing exclusion clause.

expectations.text: fix.md contains a rewritten description that includes at least TWO specific trigger keywords from the user's stated scope (any two of: 'OpenAPI', 'function signatures', 'parameter tables', 'type signatures', 'API reference') AND an explicit 'NOT for' clause naming at least TWO excluded categories from the user's list (any two of: blog posts, marketing copy, README, README files, general writing).
expectations.evidence: fix.md YAML frontmatter contains: 'OpenAPI/Swagger specs, function signatures, parameter tables, type signatures' (4 of 5 required keywords). Explicit NOT clause reads: 'Do NOT trigger for blog posts, marketing copy, README files, prose guides, tutorials, changelogs, or general writing tasks.' — covers blog posts, marketing copy, README files, and general writing (4 of 5 required exclusions).

expectations.text: fix.md recommends a concrete activation-testing approach, satisfied by EITHER (a) naming a skill-foundry script by filename — one of `test_activation.py`, `quick_validate.py`, `validate_skill.py`, `analyze_skill.py` — OR (b) listing at least 3 positive trigger prompts AND at least 2 negative trigger prompts to test against.
expectations.evidence: No skill-foundry script is named by filename. However, fix.md lists 6 labeled 'True-Positive Prompts (must trigger — 6/6 required)' and 8 labeled 'False-Positive Guard Prompts (must NOT trigger — 0/8 allowed)'. This satisfies option (b): ≥3 positive AND ≥2 negative trigger prompts.

expectations.text: fix.md uses at least one skill-foundry concept by name — one of: 'progressive disclosure', 'shibboleth', 'shibboleths', 'philosophy-first', 'description engineering', or 'Description Field Engineering'.
expectations.evidence: None of the required skill-foundry concept names appear in fix.md. The document uses its own framing ('false-positive magnet', 'semantic similarity', 'skill routers') but never invokes 'progressive disclosure', 'shibboleth'/'shibboleths', 'philosophy-first', 'description engineering', or 'Description Field Engineering'.
